text:
GENITOURINARY ONCOLOGY — FOLLOW-UP VISIT
Attending Physician: [Attending Oncologist]
Department: Medical Oncology, Genitourinary Oncology Program

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 71-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) presenting for scheduled 8-week follow-up since initiating Xtandi. He comes in today for PSA check, toxicity assessment, and ongoing disease management review.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Mr. Kowalski is a 71-year-old retired steelworker of Eastern European descent with a well-established history of metastatic prostate cancer, initially diagnosed approximately three years ago at age 68. At diagnosis, he presented with a PSA of 187 ng/mL and was found to have a Gleason 9 (4+5), Grade Group 5 prostate adenocarcinoma with bilateral seminal vesicle involvement, a right obturator lymph node metastasis (1.2 cm), and multiple bone metastases involving the lumbar spine at L3 and L4, the right iliac wing, and the right femoral neck. Germline testing at that time confirmed a pathogenic BRCA2 frameshift mutation (c.5946delT), consistent with hereditary BRCA2 syndrome (his family history is notable for a brother with pancreatic cancer and a sister with BRCA2-associated breast cancer).

He was initiated on androgen deprivation therapy with Lupron acetate plus abi/prednisone for metastatic hormone-sensitive disease, in line with LATITUDE and STAMPEDE intensification data. He had an excellent initial biochemical response — PSA nadir reached 0.8 ng/mL at approximately six months — and his bone scan at 18 months showed sclerotic changes consistent with treatment response at the known sites, with no new lesions. The right obturator lymph node decreased to 0.7 cm at that reassessment.

Approximately two years into his treatment course, serial PSA measurements began to rise — from a nadir of 0.8 ng/mL to 4.7 ng/mL across three consecutive measurements spaced four weeks apart — while testosterone remained firmly suppressed below 20 ng/dL. This fulfilled PCWG3 biochemical criteria for PSA progression, and the clinical diagnosis of castration-resistant prostate cancer was formally established. He also endorsed worsening low back pain at the L3-L4 level at that juncture, rated 4/10 in intensity and managed with scheduled acetaminophen.

Subsequent PSMA PET/CT (Ga-68 PSMA-11) at the time of CRPC diagnosis showed worrisome interval change: increased PSMA avidity at the known L3 and L4 vertebral lesions, plus four new PSMA-avid sites — a T10 vertebral body lesion, a right posterior 7th rib lesion, a left posterior 9th rib lesion, and a right sacral ala lesion — as well as interval enlargement of the right obturator lymph node to 1.4 cm with increased avidity. No visceral metastases were identified, and there were no PSMA-negative lesions of concern. Given this picture of bone-dominant mCRPC after Zytiga, Xtandi 160 mg orally once daily was initiated approximately eight weeks ago. leup was continued for ongoing castrate testosterone maintenance. Denosumab 120 mg subcutaneously every four weeks was also added at that visit for bone health protection, along with calcium and vitamin D supplementation.

Mr. Kowalski returns today reporting that his back pain has improved meaningfully since starting enza — he rates it approximately 2/10 and has been able to reduce his acetaminophen use to as-needed rather than scheduled dosing. He notes ongoing fatigue, which he describes as moderate, making it difficult to complete his usual household activities and short neighborhood walks without stopping to rest. He also endorses what he characterizes as "brain fog" — mild word-finding difficulty and some slowing of his thinking — which he attributes to the new medication. He denies any falls, seizures, loss of consciousness, focal neurological deficits, or chest pain. He has not noted any lower extremity weakness or saddle anesthesia. He denies dysuria, hematuria, or new urinary obstruction symptoms. He does report persistent hot flashes, approximately five to six per day, which he attributes to the ongoing Lupron. He has otherwise been able to remain at home with his wife and maintain most instrumental activities of daily living with some assistance.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Positive: Fatigue (moderate, Grade 2), mild cognitive slowing/word-finding difficulty (Grade 1), hot flashes (Grade 1, ADT-related), mild low back discomfort (markedly improved from prior, now Grade 1).
Negative: No fever, chills, or night sweats. No nausea, vomiting, or significant appetite loss. No diarrhea or constipation. No dyspnea at rest or orthopnea. No palpitations. No new lower extremity edema. No hematuria, dysuria, or urinary retention. No focal neurological symptoms, weakness, or paresthesias. No falls or seizures since starting enza. No new bone pain other than improving back pain. No right upper quadrant discomfort or jaundice.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY
1. Metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC), Gleason 9 (4+5), Grade Group 5, with bone (L3, L4, T10, bilateral ribs, sacrum, right femoral neck/iliac wing) and lymph node (right obturator) metastases — currently on enza + Lupron + denosumab
2. Germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant (c.5946delT, p.Ser1982Argfs*22) — hereditary BRCA2 syndrome
3. Hypertension — managed with amlodipine and previously required medication adjustment during Zytiga therapy
4. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — well-controlled, on oral agents
5. Hyperlipidemia — on statin

SOCIAL HISTORY
Retired steelworker, married, lives with his wife in the community. Eastern European descent. Lifelong non-smoker. No alcohol or illicit drug use. Two adult children who are supportive and accompany him to some appointments. Functional baseline prior to cancer diagnosis was ECOG 0 with full independent activity.

FAMILY HISTORY
Brother: pancreatic cancer (deceased)
Sister: breast cancer, BRCA2-associated
Family history strongly consistent with hereditary BRCA2 syndrome (genetic counseling completed at time of diagnosis).

ALLERGIES
No known drug allergies.

MEDICATIONS
1. enza (Xtandi) 160 mg orally once daily — initiated approximately 8 weeks ago for mCRPC
2. leup acetate (Lupron Depot) 22.5 mg intramuscular every 3 months — ongoing ADT, last injection given at CRPC transition visit
3. Denosumab (Xgeva) 120 mg subcutaneous every 4 weeks — initiated at CRPC transition, bone protection
4. Calcium carbonate 500 mg orally twice daily — bone support
5. Vitamin D3 1000 IU orally daily — bone support
6. Amlodipine 10 mg orally daily — hypertension
7. Lisinopril 20 mg orally daily — hypertension (dose adjusted during prior Zytiga therapy)
8. Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily — type 2 diabetes
9. Atorvastatin 40 mg orally nightly — hyperlipidemia
10. Acetaminophen 500-1000 mg orally as needed (now using less frequently) — pain management

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
Vital Signs: Blood pressure 138/82 mmHg (adequate, within acceptable range on current antihypertensives), heart rate 74 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, temperature 98.2°F (36.8°C), oxygen saturation 97% on room air, weight 181 lbs (down approximately 2 lbs from last visit — patient attributes to mild appetite change), BMI 26.8 kg/m².

General: Mr. Kowalski is a 71-year-old male who appears mildly fatigued but is alert, oriented, and in no acute distress. He is well-groomed and conversational, though he pauses occasionally during conversation — consistent with his reported mild cognitive slowing.

Head, Eyes, Ears, Nose, Throat: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Pupils equal, round, reactive to light. Sclera anicteric. Mucous membranes moist. No oral lesions.

Neck: Supple, no lymphadenopathy, no thyromegaly, no JVD.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, normal S1 and S2, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Peripheral pulses 2+ throughout. No lower extremity edema.

Respiratory: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rhonchi, or rales. Breathing unlabored.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Bowel sounds normoactive. No hepatomegaly or splenomegaly appreciated. No palpable masses. No right upper quadrant tenderness.

Musculoskeletal: Mild tenderness to deep palpation over the lower lumbar spine (L3-L4 region), unchanged or improved from prior visit. No midline spine tenderness at thoracic levels. No new focal bone tenderness at ribs or right hip today, though he reports mild right hip ache at baseline. Full range of motion at knees and hips without major limitation. Gait is steady without assistive device, though somewhat slow and deliberate.

Neurological: Alert and oriented x3. Cranial nerves II through XII intact. Motor strength 5/5 in all four extremities. Sensation intact to light touch in bilateral lower extremities. Reflexes 2+ and symmetric. No evidence of myelopathy. No ataxia on finger-nose testing. Romberg negative.

Skin: No rash, jaundice, or significant pallor. No ecchymoses suggesting coagulopathy.

Lymph nodes: No palpable inguinal, axillary, or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy on examination.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS
PSA: 1.9 ng/mL (down from 4.7 ng/mL at enza initiation — a 59% decline from baseline; nadir pending further monitoring)
Testosterone: < 20 ng/dL (castrate range confirmed, maintained on Lupron)
Complete Blood Count:
  - Hemoglobin: 11.4 g/dL (mild anemia, likely multifactorial: ADT-related, chronic disease; stable from last check)
  - Hematocrit: 34.1%
  - White blood cell count: 5.8 K/uL (within normal limits)
  - Platelet count: 212 K/uL (within normal limits)
Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
  - Sodium: 138 mEq/L, Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L, Bicarbonate: 24 mEq/L, BUN: 18 mg/dL, Creatinine: 1.1 mg/dL (eGFR ~68 mL/min/1.73m², stable CKD stage 2)
  - AST: 28 U/L, ALT: 22 U/L, Total bilirubin: 0.7 mg/dL, Alkaline phosphatase: 118 U/L (mildly elevated, bone metastasis-related, stable from prior)
  - Albumin: 3.8 g/dL
  - Glucose: 112 mg/dL (fasting, acceptable diabetic control)
Lipid panel: LDL 78 mg/dL, HDL 42 mg/dL, Triglycerides 138 mg/dL (acceptable on atorvastatin)
HbA1c (recent): 7.1% (well-controlled diabetes)

---

IMAGING RESULTS
No new imaging obtained at this visit. Prior PSMA PET/CT (performed at time of CRPC diagnosis, approximately 8 weeks ago) is the most recent cross-sectional/functional imaging study. That scan demonstrated multiple PSMA-avid bone lesions (L3, L4, T10, bilateral posterior ribs, right sacral ala, right femoral neck region) and an enlarged right obturator lymph node at 1.4 cm. No visceral metastases were identified at that time. Restaging imaging will be planned at approximately the 5-6 month mark on enza per standard practice. The meaningful PSA decline observed today is reassuring as an early indicator of biochemical response.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. Metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) — early biochemical response to Xtandi.
Mr. Kowalski has achieved a significant early PSA response on Xtandi, with PSA declining from 4.7 ng/mL at baseline to 1.9 ng/mL today — a 59% reduction at the 8-week mark. Testosterone remains in the castrate range. This is an encouraging early response. His back pain has also improved considerably, consistent with a meaningful anti-tumor effect at the bone disease sites. We will continue Xtandi at the current dose of 160 mg daily. No dose adjustment is warranted at this time. Lupron injections will be maintained on schedule for continuous testosterone suppression. Repeat PSA and labs will be obtained in approximately 6-8 weeks. Formal restaging with bone scan and CT (versus PSMA PET/CT) will be planned at approximately 5-6 months to objectively assess radiographic response.

I discussed with Mr. Kowalski that while early data are promising, the CRPC disease state often evolves resistance over time. Given his germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant with confirmed somatic second hit (loss of heterozygosity on prior tissue NGS), Lynparza (Lynparza) is a well-established next-line option upon Xtandi progression, based on the PROfound trial. This has been discussed as part of our longitudinal planning and the patient is aware. No PARP inhibitor is indicated at present while enza is controlling disease.

2. enza-related fatigue — Grade 2, manageable.
The patient's Grade 2 fatigue is a well-recognized and expected adverse effect of Xtandi. It is somewhat compounded by ongoing ADT-related fatigue and mild anemia. At this time, it does not warrant dose reduction given the excellent early PSA response and the patient's overall ECOG 1 performance status. I encouraged him to engage with a physical therapy program — light aerobic exercise (walking, low-impact activity) is one of the most evidence-based interventions for cancer-related fatigue and may also reduce the risk of falls and sarcopenia in the context of ADT-related muscle loss. A formal PT referral was placed today.

3. Xtandi-related cognitive effects — Grade 1, monitoring.
Mild cognitive slowing is a recognized CNS-related adverse effect of Xtandi, particularly in older patients. His symptoms are currently Grade 1 (subjective slowing, mild word-finding, no impact on major ADLs). I counseled him to avoid driving if he feels cognitively impaired at any point and to notify us of any escalation. Formal neuropsychological evaluation is not yet indicated given the mild and expected nature of his symptoms. His wife was present for this discussion and will help monitor.

4. Fall risk — counseling and safety.
Although Mr. Kowalski has not fallen since starting enza, his age (71 years), ongoing ADT (which contributes to muscle weakness, reduced proprioception, and postural hypotension), fatigue, and mild cognitive effects all contribute to elevated fall risk. We discussed fall prevention measures: ensuring the home environment is clear of trip hazards, adequate lighting at night, use of handrails on stairs, and avoidance of activities requiring quick reflexes in the setting of any cognitive slowing. Physical therapy referral above will also address balance and gait safety.

5. Bone metastasis management — denosumab continued.
Denosumab (Xgeva) 120 mg subcutaneous every 4 weeks is being continued for bone health protection in the setting of extensive osseous metastatic disease. Calcium and vitamin D supplementation ongoing. I reminded the patient of the importance of dental hygiene and to notify us before any invasive dental procedures given the rare but serious risk of medication-related osteonecrosis of the jaw (MRONJ) with denosumab. He has not had any dental work recently and was advised to have a routine dental cleaning but to hold off on any tooth extractions without discussion with our office first. He reported no jaw pain, loose teeth, or oral lesions today.

6. Hypertension — acceptable blood pressure control.
Blood pressure today at 138/82 mmHg on amlodipine 10 mg and lisinopril 20 mg. This is acceptable. We will continue current antihypertensive regimen. Note that blood pressure management was more challenging during the prior Zytiga-containing regimen; enza does not carry the same mineralocorticoid excess effect, and blood pressure has remained more stable since the transition.

7. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — well-controlled.
Fasting glucose 112 mg/dL, HbA1c 7.1%. Metformin continued. No adjustment needed. Renal function stable at eGFR ~68 mL/min, adequate for metformin use at current dose.

8. Mild anemia — monitoring.
Hemoglobin 11.4 g/dL, consistent with mild chronic anemia in the context of ongoing ADT and metastatic malignancy. No evidence of active bleeding or hemolysis. Iron stores not formally checked today — will reassess at next visit. No transfusion indication at this time. Will monitor CBC at each follow-up.

9. Goals of care — ongoing longitudinal discussion.
Mr. Kowalski remains highly engaged and motivated to continue active treatment. He and his family understand that the current disease state — mCRPC with extensive bone metastases — is not curable, and that treatment goals are disease control, symptom management, and quality of life preservation. He expressed optimism about the early PSA response and wishes to continue current therapy. I reinforced the importance of communicating any new or worsening symptoms — particularly any new bone pain, neurological symptoms (which could suggest cord compression), or constitutional deterioration — promptly between scheduled visits.

RETURN VISIT: Approximately 6-8 weeks. Sooner if symptoms worsen.

Orders placed:
- PSA, testosterone, CBC, CMP in 6 weeks
- Physical therapy referral (cancer-related fatigue, fall prevention, deconditioning)
- Dental hygiene reminder communicated to patient
- Continue all current medications as listed

Time spent: Approximately 45 minutes total visit time, including more than 50% time in counseling and coordination of care.
